description: Pre-seeded experiment queue. Reordered for YouTube channel growth: fundamentals first, high-virality early, contrarian mid-season, synthesis experiments after their dependencies, live-mode flagship last. Status: queued | running | complete | filmed.
reorder_rationale: 001→013 back-to-back uses same variants immediately (no extra probe cost). 017 (contrarian) early builds trust. 012/019 (synthesis) placed after all their dependencies complete. 016 (null compilation) at week 16 when enough null data exists. 018 (JSON-LD) and 020 (live) stay late — most technical/slow.

experiments.title: Statistical Anchors
experiments.hypothesis: If the opening sentence contains a specific number ('cut latency 43%'), then citation rate will be higher than a version with vague language ('improved latency significantly'), because LLMs weight precise, citable data points as credibility signals.
experiments.variable: Presence of a specific statistic in the opening sentence
experiments.youtube_angle: The most fundamental GEO claim tested for the first time with real data.

experiments.title: Engine Divergence Special
experiments.hypothesis: The four engines (GPT-4o, Gemini, Perplexity, Claude) will disagree on which variant wins for at least one lever, revealing engine-specific citation biases rather than a universal principle.
experiments.variable: Engine identity (cross-engine comparison using 001 variants, not a content variable)
experiments.note: Re-run 001 variants but report by engine. Zero extra content work — reuses 001 design.
experiments.youtube_angle: Engine Wars episode — 'ChatGPT cites this. Perplexity ignores it.' Most shareable early video.

experiments.title: Answer-First Structure
experiments.hypothesis: If the citable conclusion appears in sentence 1 ('L8EntSpace reduces AI invisibility by 40%'), then citation rate will be higher than burying it in paragraph 3, because LLMs weight content that front-loads the answer.
experiments.variable: Position of the citable claim (sentence 1 vs paragraph 3)
experiments.youtube_angle: Structural writing advice with data behind it — broad appeal beyond GEO.

experiments.title: Definition Sentences
experiments.hypothesis: If content uses 'X is a Y that does Z' definitional structure, then citation rate will be higher than equivalent hedged prose, because definitions are citation magnets — models reach for them when answering 'what is X' queries.
experiments.variable: Definitional sentence structure vs hedged narrative prose
experiments.youtube_angle: One sentence structure change that makes AI quote you — highly actionable.

experiments.title: Opening Question vs Opening Statement
experiments.hypothesis: If content opens by restating the query as a direct question ('What is the best way to get cited by AI?'), then citation rate will be lower than content that opens with the answer — because LLMs prefer answer-mode sources, not question-mode sources.
experiments.variable: Opening sentence structure (question vs declarative answer)
experiments.note: Contrarian — predicts the control (A) wins. Great 'I was wrong about this' angle.
experiments.youtube_angle: Counterintuitive result builds credibility. Most content advice says 'start with a question' — this tests it.

experiments.title: List vs Prose
experiments.hypothesis: If facts are presented as a bulleted list, then citation rate will be higher than equivalent paragraph prose, because list items are discrete, copy-pasteable, and easier for LLMs to extract and attribute.
experiments.variable: Content format: bulleted list vs flowing paragraph
experiments.youtube_angle: Instantly actionable. Everyone can restructure their content as bullets today.

experiments.title: Source Framing: First-Person vs Third-Party
experiments.hypothesis: If claims are framed as third-party attributions ('According to independent testing…'), then citation rate will be higher than first-person brand claims ('We found that…'), because LLMs trust attributed claims over self-promotion.
experiments.variable: Claim attribution voice (third-party vs first-person brand)
experiments.youtube_angle: Surprising implication: writing about yourself in third person may actually work.

experiments.title: Entity Density
experiments.hypothesis: If content names 3+ concrete entities (products, people, standards, organisations) per 100 words, then citation rate will be higher than sparse entity usage, because entity density makes content more 'graspable' to retrieval systems.
experiments.variable: Number of named concrete entities per 100 words
experiments.youtube_angle: Name-dropping as a strategy — counterintuitive but grounded in how retrieval works.

experiments.title: Freshness Signals
experiments.hypothesis: If content includes explicit date markers ('As of 2026…', 'Updated June 2026'), then citation rate will be higher than undated equivalent content, because LLMs prefer temporally anchored sources for factual queries.
experiments.variable: Presence of date markers (dated vs undated)
experiments.youtube_angle: Does adding a year to your content make AI cite it more? Simple test, big implications.

experiments.title: Short Punchy Sentences vs Compound
experiments.hypothesis: If each claim is a single short sentence (≤15 words), then citation rate will be higher than the same claims expressed in long compound sentences, because short sentences are more quote-able in LLM answers.
experiments.variable: Sentence length (≤15 words vs 30+ words per sentence)
experiments.youtube_angle: Copywriting meets GEO — cross-audience appeal with content creators and marketers.

experiments.title: Contradiction Handling
experiments.hypothesis: If content explicitly addresses and refutes a common false claim about the topic, then citation rate will be higher than content that ignores competing narratives, because LLMs prioritise sources that resolve ambiguity.
experiments.variable: Presence of explicit contradiction handling vs silence on alternatives
experiments.youtube_angle: The 'myth-busting' content strategy tested with data.

experiments.title: Claim Specificity
experiments.hypothesis: If claims use highly specific metrics ('reduced median API latency from 340ms to 197ms'), then citation rate will be higher than category-level claims ('significantly improved API performance'), because specificity signals measurability.
experiments.variable: Level of specificity (precise metric vs category descriptor)
experiments.youtube_angle: Extension of the statistics experiment — tests whether the *format* of precision matters.

experiments.title: Brand Name Frequency
experiments.hypothesis: If the brand name appears 4+ times in 200 words, then citation rate will be higher than content where it appears once, because repetition strengthens the signal connecting the brand to the topic.
experiments.variable: Brand name repetition count (1× vs 4+× per 200 words)
experiments.youtube_angle: Does keyword stuffing (for your own brand name) actually work in the AI era?

experiments.title: Active vs Passive Voice
experiments.hypothesis: If claims use active voice ('L8EntSpace reduced onboarding time by 60%'), then citation rate will be higher than passive voice equivalents ('Onboarding time was reduced by 60%'), because active constructions are more attribution-ready.
experiments.variable: Grammatical voice (active vs passive)
experiments.youtube_angle: Writing rule every editor knows — tested against AI citation data for the first time.

experiments.title: Heading Hierarchy
experiments.hypothesis: If content uses H2/H3 heading hierarchy to label facts, then citation rate will be higher than flat prose without headings, because structural markup gives retrieval systems clear extraction anchors.
experiments.variable: Presence of H2/H3 heading structure vs unstructured prose
experiments.youtube_angle: Does document structure affect AI citation? Tests a universal content recommendation.

experiments.title: Null Results Compilation
experiments.hypothesis: Multiple levers tested individually will show no statistically significant effect, revealing which popular GEO advice is unsupported by data — a null result is a valid, publishable finding.
experiments.variable: Compilation episode — no new probe, re-analyzes past non-significant results
experiments.note: Run this after ~14 experiments. Re-analyzes existing data — no probe cost. High trust-building value.
experiments.youtube_angle: The 'myth-busting' compilation. 'I tested 14 GEO tactics. Half of them do nothing.' Very shareable.

experiments.title: JSON-LD Schema Presence
experiments.hypothesis: If source content includes Organisation + FAQPage JSON-LD schema markup, then citation rate on retrieval-grounded engines (Perplexity, Gemini) will be higher than plain-text equivalent, because structured markup aids retrieval parsing.
experiments.variable: Presence of JSON-LD schema (structured vs unstructured)
experiments.note: Perplexity and Gemini grounding only. More technical episode — strong for SEO/technical audience.
experiments.youtube_angle: Does JSON-LD actually get you cited? Technical test that SEOs have wanted for years.

experiments.title: Anchor Stacking (Multi-Lever Combined)
experiments.hypothesis: If content combines the three highest-performing individual levers (statistical anchor + definition sentence + answer-first), then citation rate will be higher than a control with none of these — and higher than any single lever alone.
experiments.variable: Combination of top 3 proven levers vs control
experiments.note: Depends on 001, 002, 003 being complete. The 'putting it all together' episode.
experiments.youtube_angle: Season finale energy — combining the proven winners. Practical synthesis video.

experiments.title: Statistical Anchor × Content Length Interaction
experiments.hypothesis: The citation lift from a statistical anchor in the opening sentence is larger in short-form content (150 words) than in long-form (600 words), because the anchor's relative weight diminishes when it is a smaller fraction of the total content.
experiments.variable: Statistical anchor combined with content length (short vs long)
experiments.note: Depends on 001 and 006 being complete. Investigates interaction effects between two proven levers.
experiments.youtube_angle: Advanced episode for the engaged audience — 'does content length change the rules?'

experiments.title: Schema Scope: Site-Wide vs Page-Scoped JSON-LD
experiments.hypothesis: If FAQPage + Organisation JSON-LD is published on every page of a site (root layout), then citation rate will be higher than the same schema published only on the /faq page, because AI crawlers may land on any page and will ingest the structured data regardless of entry point — whereas page-scoped markup is only discoverable if the crawler happens to visit that one URL.
experiments.variable: Scope of JSON-LD injection: root layout (all pages) vs /faq page only
experiments.primary_metric: AI citation rate on Perplexity + Gemini (the upside)
guardrail_metrics: Google Search Console: structured-data error/warning count for FAQPage items (must not increase)
GSC manual actions report: must remain clean (a 'structured data' manual action is an immediate fail + rollback)
GSC Performance: organic impressions, clicks, and average position for the site's top 20 tracked queries (must not regress beyond noise)
Rich-result eligibility for /faq (page-scoped variant is the legitimate home for FAQ rich results; site-wide must not cannibalise or disqualify it)
Crawl stats: Googlebot crawl-budget shift from the extra per-page payload
experiments.decision_rule: Site-wide (B) only 'wins' if it lifts AI citation rate AND holds all guardrails flat. If citation rate rises but any SEO guardrail regresses, the finding is a TRADE-OFF, not a win — report both sides and recommend the page-scoped variant unless the citation lift is large enough to justify the SEO cost.
experiments.rollback_trigger: Any GSC structured-data manual action, or a sustained (>2 week) organic-position regression on tracked queries, triggers immediate revert to page-scoped schema.
experiments.note: Live-mode only — fast in-context probes cannot distinguish where the JSON-LD appeared, nor can they observe SEO side-effects. Requires GSC access on the test property and a 4-week+ re-probe/observation window. Builds on experiment 018 (JSON-LD presence vs absence) and 020 (live-mode baseline); run after both. NOTE: Google has restricted FAQ rich results to authoritative gov/health sites since 2023, so the Google-side upside is largely gone and this experiment is mostly about the AI-crawler upside vs the SEO-policy downside. This two-sided framing is the whole point — a citation win that costs organic traffic is not a win.
experiments.youtube_angle: The definitive experiment — live content, real indexing, 6-week wait. Channel milestone video.
